evidence: Production files define large sets of package-level function-variable seams (for example `goseg/docker/wireguard.go`, `goseg/docker/minio.go`, `goseg/routines/docker.go`, `goseg/routines/version.go`) that are overridden in tests, adding an indirection layer to normal runtime code.

Wireguard recovery/restart choreography is duplicated across modules (`goseg/handler/startram.go` and `goseg/routines/docker.go`) with nearly identical steps: discover wireguard ships, restart wireguard, stop/delete per-ship containers, delete minio sidecars, and reload urbit/mc/minio.

Global `config.Conf()` (full `SysConfig` bag) is read directly by many modules (`goseg/main.go`, `goseg/handler/startram.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `goseg/docker/urbit.go`) instead of narrow contracts, so module dependencies are broader than behavior requires.

README.md defines a backend quality gate that requires deterministic unit tests for upload-path changes, but the contract is procedural only and not tied to any executable check in assigned runtime files.

goseg/handler/upload.go contains branch-critical action routing (`open-endpoint`, `reset`, default error) with injected service seams but no in-file test references or documented required test cases for action matrix and error wrapping behavior.

goseg/startram/errors.go implements security-sensitive redaction/wrapping behavior (`maskPubkey` + `Unwrap`) without any test contract mention in README.md for redaction regressions or cause-chain guarantees.

`goseg/handler/newship.go` and `goseg/importer/importer.go` duplicate near-identical ship-ready polling, remote toggle waiting, Startram registration, and cleanup orchestration with only transition types/messages differing.

`goseg/handler/urbit_operations.go` repeats the same stop/reconfigure/start/wait/recover lifecycle in `ChopPier`, `rollChopPier`, and `packMeldPier`, including repeated transition publish and restart restoration logic.
`goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go:150-394` (`ConstructPierInfo`) combines filesystem backup discovery, docker state reads, network/service checks, click desk probes, and DTO assembly in one function.
`goseg/config/config.go:60-173` performs process bootstrap, key generation, config migration, hashing, persistence, and goroutine startup in `init()`, creating broad startup coupling.
`goseg/system/wifi.go:117-282` mixes system command orchestration, AP lifecycle, websocket API handling, and client broadcast loops in one module.

findings.summary: Test seam strategy relies on runtime-global function variables, creating pervasive low-value indirection.
evidence: `goseg/docker/wireguard.go` declares many aliases like `startContainerForWireguard = StartContainer`, `getLatestContainerInfoForWireguard = GetLatestContainerInfo`, `confForWireguard = config.Conf`.
`goseg/docker/minio.go` and `goseg/routines/docker.go` follow the same pattern (`confForMinIO`, `startContainerForMinIO`, `getContainerStateFn`, `updateWgOnFn`, etc.), and tests mutate those globals.
This pattern makes call paths harder to follow in production and introduces shared mutable seams that couple runtime code structure to test setup.
findings.suggestion: Replace package-global seam variables with explicit dependency structs/interfaces constructed once per module (for example a `DockerRuntime`/`VersionRuntime` with concrete defaults). Keep seam override capability in tests via constructor injection instead of mutable package globals.

findings.summary: Wireguard restart/recovery flow is copy-pasted across handlers and routines instead of abstracted once.
evidence: `handleStartramRestart` in `goseg/handler/startram.go` builds `wgShips`, restarts wireguard, stops ships (`click.BarExit`/`WaitForShipExit`), deletes ship+minio containers, then calls `docker.LoadUrbits`, `docker.LoadMC`, and `docker.LoadMinIOs`.
`Check502Loop` in `goseg/routines/docker.go` repeats the same orchestration sequence when a second 502 strike occurs.
Both implementations encode the same policy but diverge in error aggregation/reporting style, increasing drift risk when restart behavior changes.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single orchestrator (for example `docker.RecoverWireguardFleet(ctx, opts)`) that owns ship discovery, stop/delete/recreate sequencing, and error policy. Call it from both `handleStartramRestart` and `Check502Loop` with scenario-specific options.

findings.summary: Modules depend on full mutable `SysConfig` instead of narrow settings contracts.
evidence: `config.Conf()` returns full `structs.SysConfig` (`goseg/config/config.go`), and call sites across modules pull only subsets (for example `WgRegistered`, `WgOn`, `Piers`, `SnapTime`).
`goseg/main.go`, `goseg/handler/startram.go`, and `goseg/importer/importer.go` each read broad config state for orchestration decisions, coupling unrelated logic to a monolithic bag.
`goseg/config/config.go` already defines narrower snapshots (`StartramSettingsSnapshot`, `ShipSettingsSnapshot`, `RuntimeSettingsSnapshot`) but core callers still use `Conf()` directly.
findings.suggestion: Adopt narrow read models at boundaries: replace direct `Conf()` calls in orchestration paths with dedicated snapshot/accessor APIs per concern (startram, ships, runtime). Phase out `Conf()` usage in non-config packages except where full state is genuinely required.

findings.summary: Container bootstrap implementations repeat the same skeleton without a shared abstraction.
evidence: `LoadWireguard` and `LoadNetdata` both implement: check/open config file, create default if missing, write generated config, start container, update container state.
`LoadMC` in `goseg/docker/minio.go` applies the same startup skeleton with minor policy differences (registration gate and names).
The repeated skeleton causes policy and error-handling behavior to be scattered across multiple loaders rather than represented as one reusable boot contract.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared loader pipeline abstraction (for example `bootService(spec)` with hooks for preconditions, default-file creation, config materialization, container start, and state update) and migrate wireguard/netdata/mc loaders to it.

findings.summary: Shipcreator’s config interface is a mostly pass-through wrapper with one concrete implementation.
evidence: `ConfigService` in `goseg/shipcreator/shipcreator.go` is implemented only by `configService`, whose methods directly forward to `config` package functions (`Conf`, `UrbitConf`, `UrbitConfAll`, `UpdateUrbitConfig`, `UpdateConfTyped`).
This interface adds call indirection but little policy/translation logic; the abstraction boundary does not represent a distinct domain model.
A package-global `defaultService` further hides construction and keeps static coupling to global config behavior.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the pass-through interface and call `config` directly, or strengthen the abstraction by introducing a true repository/service contract with focused methods that encode ship-creation invariants (ports, persistence, pier list mutation) instead of forwarding raw config operations.

findings.summary: Upload-path testing is documented as required but not encoded as an enforceable quality gate.
evidence: README.md states upload-path changes should include at least one deterministic unit test.
README.md quality gates are command-based guidance and do not define a CI-enforced check for upload-specific coverage/contract tests.
goseg/handler/upload.go is a high-risk dispatch surface with multiple behavior branches, so relying on manual adherence creates regression risk.
findings.suggestion: Add a mandatory CI check that fails when upload-path files change without corresponding targeted tests (for example via path-aware test selection, changed-files policy, or coverage threshold scoped to handler/upload flows), and document that rule in README.md.

findings.summary: Security-relevant redaction behavior lacks explicit test strategy coverage in project contracts.
evidence: goseg/startram/errors.go constructs externally visible masked errors while preserving internal cause chains via `Unwrap()`.
README.md backend quality gate does not specify redaction/PII-safety assertions or error-contract tests for masking functions.
A regression in message masking or unwrap behavior would not be specifically caught by the documented gate.
findings.suggestion: Extend the backend quality gate documentation to require explicit tests for error redaction contracts (masked external message, preserved internal cause), and add package-level tests covering leak-prevention edge cases.

findings.summary: Action-routing logic has no documented branch test matrix, increasing fragility when actions evolve.
evidence: goseg/handler/upload.go routes behavior by string actions and has three outcome classes: `open-endpoint`, `reset`, and default error.
README.md requires generic tests for upload paths but does not define branch-level expectations (unknown action handling, payload decode failure, service error propagation).
Without a required matrix, future action additions can silently omit negative-path assertions.
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce a minimal branch-matrix test checklist for action handlers (decode failure, each supported action success/failure, unsupported action), and tie it to PR/CI policy for handler action surfaces.

findings.summary: New-ship and import flows duplicate the same asynchronous lifecycle orchestration, causing parallel logic drift risk.
evidence: `waitForShipReady` in `newship.go` (lines 173-226) and `importer.go` (lines 577-643) both poll `click.GetLusCode`, conditionally switch to wireguard, delete/restart containers, and finalize transitions.
`newShipToggleRemote` (lines 228-246) and `importShipToggleRemote` (lines 645-663) are effectively the same loop with different transition publishers.
`newShipRegisterService` (lines 284-288) and `registerServices` (lines 786-790) duplicate the same Startram registration call.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `ShipProvisioningOrchestrator` (phase runner + transition adapter) used by both new-ship and import flows, with pluggable transition emitters and optional phases (e.g., extraction/fix-acme).

findings.summary: Urbit maintenance operations reimplement the same multi-step container lifecycle instead of using a common operation primitive.
evidence: `ChopPier` (471-540), `rollChopPier` (980-1063), and `packMeldPier` (295-380+) all perform status check, conditional stop with `|exit`, `WaitComplete`, boot-status mutation, restart, and transition publish cycles.
These functions duplicate error-wrapper closures and delayed transition cleanup (`time.Sleep` + publish empty event), which increases divergence surface for behavior fixes.
`WaitComplete` exists as shared polling in `urbit.go` (147-190), but higher-level maintenance choreography remains copy-pasted.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a reusable maintenance state-machine helper that accepts ordered phases (`stop`, `run bootStatus`, `wait`, `restore`) and transition metadata, then have chop/roll/pack-meld compose phase lists.

findings.summary: Broadcast state construction is concentrated in one large function that mixes unrelated responsibilities.
evidence: `ConstructPierInfo` (150-394) performs local backup filesystem scanning, remote backup flattening, docker status/stats retrieval, click desk probing, Startram subdomain readiness checks, and UI struct assembly in one routine.
The function reads from config, docker, click, filesystem, and cached broadcast state in one pass, making behavior changes cross-cutting and hard to test in isolation.
The same function contains domain normalization logic (pack day/date defaults, alias visibility, backup projections), not just data collection.
findings.suggestion: Split into composable providers: `backupSnapshot`, `runtimeSnapshot`, `startramSnapshot`, and `urbitViewAssembler`; keep `ConstructPierInfo` as thin coordination only.

findings.summary: Domain handlers directly embed websocket/UI transition protocol details, coupling business orchestration to transport semantics.
evidence: Multiple handlers inline repeated event lifecycle patterns (`Publish... loading/error/success`, `time.Sleep`, clear) instead of a shared transition transaction helper.

`startram.go` repeats local `handleError` closures in separate actions (`handleStartramRegister`, `handleStartramEndpoint`, `handleStartramCancel`) with slightly different event types and reset behavior.
`support.go` and `newship.go` also encode transition timing and cleanup sequencing directly in business flow, making orchestration logic depend on UI event choreography.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared transition-emitter utility (start/progress/fail/succeed/clear with policy-driven delays) and keep handlers focused on domain steps + typed outcomes.

findings.summary: Configuration package combines initialization, migration, key management, runtime state, and utility concerns in one central module.
evidence: `config.go` is 1205 lines and `init()` (60-173) performs filesystem creation, key setup, merge/migration, hash computation, persistence, and starts a goroutine (`ConfChannel`).
The package also stores mutable runtime registries (`GSContainers`, `startramConfig`, minio password map) alongside disk persistence and random/hash helpers.
Sibling files (`urbit.go`, `version.go`, `mc.go`) use better-scoped persistence helpers, but `config.go` remains a catch-all orchestration center.
findings.suggestion: Split `config` into explicit subpackages/components: `bootstrap`, `store` (disk persistence), `runtime_state`, and `credentials`; make initialization explicit from `main` instead of heavy `init()` side effects.

findings.summary: WiFi module mixes OS command orchestration, AP lifecycle, websocket request handling, and periodic broadcast loops in one package surface.
evidence: `C2CMode`, `C2CConnect`, and `UnaliveC2C` orchestrate system services and shell commands, while `CaptiveAPI` handles websocket protocol parsing and `announceNetworks` manages long-lived client broadcasts in the same file.
`C2CConnect` includes system reboot side effects (`execCommandForWiFi("reboot")`), while `CaptiveAPI` may also restart groundseg, indicating competing lifecycle paths in transport-facing code.
Shared mutable `clients` map is managed in the same module that executes networking shell commands, increasing responsibility spread.
findings.suggestion: Separate WiFi domain service (connect/AP state transitions) from captive websocket transport handlers and from network-announcement publisher; expose a narrow service API consumed by handlers.
